---
title: "Overview: CO2-FT, Fe3O4, and Microkinetics"
---

# Overview

CO2 Fischer-Tropsch (CO2-FT) chemistry couples CO2 activation with hydrogenation and carbon-chain growth. Iron catalysts are attractive because they can support RWGS activity, carbide formation, and hydrocarbon synthesis under related reaction environments.

For this course, Fe3O4 is treated as a starting point for learning how oxide surfaces participate in CO2 activation and water formation. Later lessons can connect this oxide-centered view to Fe carbide phases and oxide-carbide interfaces.

## Modeling Habit

Before fitting rates or interpreting catalyst design, every mechanism should pass three checks:

- Species are defined clearly.
- Site balance is conserved.
- Elemental mass balance is conserved for every elementary step.

These checks do not prove that a mechanism is true, but they prevent many avoidable modeling mistakes.
